docs.title: Procure — Darcio's Work
docs.text: Project: The Organization of Corruption in Public Procurement Authors: Sergio Galletta (ETH Zurich), Darcio Genicolo Martins (Insper), Henrik Sigstad (BI Norwegian Business School) Last updated: March 2026

docs.title: What this site contains
docs.text: This site consolidates all empirical work by Darcio on the NLL identification strategy (Pillar 2), including data pipelines, estimation results, referee simulations, and discussion topics for co-authors.

docs.title: Key findings
docs.text:
Mechanism taxonomy validated (sign test p = 0.035)

7 of 8 directional predictions from the Santos & Souza (2024) mechanism taxonomy are confirmed under NLL adoption. The taxonomy predicts corruption composition shifts better than chance.

Decreto 9.412 placebo: p < 0.001

The 2018 threshold change produces massive bunching migration (3/3 predictions correct). This validates the mechanism with an independent reform event and should be the paper's first empirical result.

Individual outcomes underpowered

No individual scheme outcome is significant at 5%. Two approach significance: dispensa_indevida (+6.9 pp, p = 0.092) and inexecução (+2.7 pp, p = 0.055). Audit lag and small comparison group (65 never-treated) are the bottlenecks.

Process-level DiD: informative null

28,792 NLL vs Lei 8.666 processes within the same municipality-year show no difference on observables. The NLL changes corruption composition, not process-level efficiency.

docs.title: Three decisions needed
docs.text:
1. Paper framing

Is this a paper about validating the mechanism taxonomy (sign test p = 0.035 + Decreto 9.412 p < 0.001)? Or about null effects that are directionally consistent?

2. Decreto 9.412 placement

Main result (Figure 1) or robustness check? Leading with mechanism validation changes the paper structure.

3. Number of pillars

Two (enforcement + rules) or three (+ political actors)? Pillar 3 first stage is weak (Bolsonaro alignment ≠ NLL adoption speed). Donor pipeline infeasible (corporate donations banned 2015).

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Python 3.12+ with: pandas, numpy, pyarrow, linearmodels, csdid, drdid, scipy
  • R 4.x with: did, HonestDiD, ggplot2
  • Stata 19 with: csdid, drdid (from SSC)

docs.title: Step-by-step
docs.text:
cd Darcio/data/Audesp/

# Step 0: Raw data must be present (73 licitacao-*.parquet files)
#         + adocao_14133_por_municipio_sp.csv

# Step 1: Build municipality-year panel from raw Audesp
python3 build_nll_panel.py
# Output: nll_did_panel.parquet (561 munis × 7 years)
# Time: ~5 min | Memory: ~3 GB peak

# Step 2: Prepare balanced analytic dataset
python3 prepare_analytic.py
# Output: output_csdid/data_analytic/nll_csdid_panel.{csv,dta}

# Step 3: TWFE event study (baseline)
python3 run_event_study.py
# Output: nll_event_study_coefs.csv, nll_event_study_results.json

# Step 4: Callaway-Sant'Anna (primary: R)
Rscript run_csdid_R.R
# Output: output_csdid/tables/csdid_results_R.csv + 42 figures

# Step 5: Cross-validation (Python, Stata)
python3 run_csdid_python.py
stata -b do run_csdid_stata.do

# Step 6: Diagnostics and HonestDiD
Rscript run_honestdid.R
# Output: honestdid_results.csv, pretrend_tests.csv + 14 figures

docs.title: Total runtime
docs.text: ~10 minutes end-to-end. The bottleneck is build_nll_panel.py (~5 min for 260M rows with batch processing). CS estimation takes ~2 minutes per language.

docs.title: Cross-Language Comparison
docs.text:
The analysis was implemented in three languages for robustness verification.

Language Package Version Base period default R (primary) did latest "universal" (set explicitly) Python csdid 0.2.9 "varying" Stata csdid latest (SSC) "varying"

docs.title: Spec A results by language
docs.text: Outcome R ATT Python ATT Stata ATT Max |Δ| share_pregao_eletronico +0.017 +0.039 +0.029 0.022 share_pregao_presencial −0.005 −0.018 −0.027 0.022 share_dispensa +0.013 −0.050 −0.032 0.063 share_convite −0.029 −0.031 −0.024 0.007 share_tomada −0.013 +0.009 +0.008 0.022 single_bidder_rate −0.013 +0.001 −0.002 0.014 mean_n_bidders +2.018 +2.327 +2.050 0.309

docs.title: share_dispensa (max |Δ| = 0.063)
docs.text:
The largest discrepancy arises from the base_period setting:

  • R: base_period = "universal" — uses a single reference period (typically the earliest pre-treatment period) for all cohorts
  • Python/Stata: base_period = "varying" (default) — uses the period immediately before treatment for each cohort

When pre-treatment coefficients differ across cohorts, these two approaches yield different ATT(g,t) estimates and different aggregated ATTs. This is a known design choice, not a bug.

Recommendation

Use R with base_period = "universal" as the primary result, following the recommendation in Callaway and Sant'Anna (2021) for consistent pre-trend testing.

docs.title: Other outcomes
docs.text:
Most outcomes show differences < 0.025, consistent with:

  1. Bootstrap variation (the doubly robust estimator involves resampling)
  2. Numerical precision differences across implementations
  3. Minor implementation divergences (e.g., how allow_unbalanced_panel handles missing data)

docs.title: Qualitative agreement
docs.text:
No qualitative result changes across languages

  • All three agree: no outcome significant at 5%
  • All three agree on the sign of share_convite (negative) and mean_n_bidders (positive)
  • The marginal significance of share_convite is most pronounced in R (p = 0.089), weaker in Python (p = 0.127) and Stata (p = 0.156)

docs.title: Source description
docs.text:
Audesp (Auditoria Eletrônica de Órgãos Públicos) is the electronic auditing system maintained by the Tribunal de Contas do Estado de São Paulo (TCE-SP). It receives structured data from all 645 municipalities in São Paulo state on procurement processes (licitações), contracts, spending commitments, and budget execution.

The procurement data used here covers bid-level records for all competitive and non-competitive processes published through the Audesp portal, spanning 2018 through December 2024.

docs.title: Acquisition and conversion
docs.text:
Raw data were obtained as semicolon-delimited CSV files organized by month, then batch-converted to Parquet using csv_to_parquet.py.

Why Parquet?

  • ~80% compression vs. CSV (storage and I/O)
  • Columnar format enables reading only needed columns (4 of 21 in our case)
  • Typed columns eliminate repeated type inference on every load

The converter uses:

  • Chunked reading calibrated to available RAM (psutil)
  • Parallel processing (ProcessPoolExecutor)
  • Encoding detection (chardet) with latin-1 fallback
  • Resumable design (skips already-converted files)

docs.title: Raw data summary
docs.text: Period Files Rows Notes 2018 1 (annual) 19,743,670 Single file, 126 MB Parquet 2019 12 (monthly) 25,946,078 2020 12 (monthly) 23,929,634 2021 12 (monthly) 32,221,460 NLL enacted (Apr 2021) 2022 12 (monthly) 57,045,967 Sep file has 22.5M rows 2023 12 (monthly) 38,627,134 2024 12 (monthly) 62,508,149 Total 73 ~260,022,143

docs.title: Column schema (21 columns)
docs.text: Column Type Description Município string Municipality name Entidade string Procuring entity Código da Licitação int64/string Unique process identifier Modalidade de licitação string Procurement modality (408 raw variants) Objeto string Procurement object description Produto (item) string Item being procured Quantidade/Valor fields string Estimated quantities and unit prices Número/Data do edital string Notice number and date CNPJ do participante string Bidder's tax ID Nome do participante string Bidder name Resultado da Habilitação string Qualification result Valor da Proposta string Bid value

docs.title: 1. Column name encoding
docs.text:
Some monthly Parquet files wrap column names in literal quotes:

Schema shows: "Município": large_string
Expected:     Município: string

Resolution: _resolve_columns() in build_nll_panel.py strips quotes and matches case-insensitively against the expected column list.

docs.title: 2. Modality name heterogeneity
docs.text:
408 distinct modality strings found across all files. Examples of the same modality:

"Pregao Eletronico"
"Pregão Eletrônico"
"PREGÃO ELETRÔNICO"
"Pregão Eletrôcnico - Compras Gov."
"pregão eletronico"

Resolution: _classify_modality() uses accent-stripped, lowercased substring matching to map all variants to 12 canonical categories. Coverage: 99.97%.

docs.title: 3. Memory constraints
docs.text:
The 2018 annual file (19.7M rows) expands to 9.5 GB in memory when all columns are loaded as strings. On a 15 GB WSL instance, this causes OOM kills.

Resolution: Batch processing reads only 4 needed columns via pyarrow.ParquetFile.iter_batches(batch_size=1_000_000), keeping peak memory at ~1.5 GB.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
The Audesp data records procurement processes by modality but does not explicitly flag which law governs each process (Lei 8.666/93 vs. Lei 14.133/2021). To establish when each municipality first adopted the NLL, we queried the Portal Nacional de Contratações Públicas (PNCP), the federal platform where NLL-governed procurements must be published.

A municipality's first PNCP publication serves as a proxy for NLL adoption.

docs.title: Method
docs.text:
Script: pncp_adocao_por_municipio.py

Queries the PNCP REST API for each of 645 SP municipalities.

docs.title: API details
docs.text:
• Endpoint: https://pncp.gov.br/api/consulta/v1/contratacoes/publicacao
  • Parameters: dataInicial, dataFinal, codigoModalidadeContratacao, codigoMunicipioIbge
  • Constraint: API rejects date ranges > 1 year (HTTP 422)

docs.title: Query strategy
docs.text:
For each municipality, the script searches:

  • 5 annual windows: 2021, 2022, 2023, 2024, 2025
  • 4 NLL-relevant modality codes: Concurso (3), Pregão Eletrônico (6), Dispensa (8), Inexigibilidade (9)

This yields ~20 API requests per municipality (~8 seconds at 0.4s rate limiting).

docs.title: Resilience features
docs.text:
• Rate limiting: 0.4s between requests
  • Timeout: 60s per request
  • Retries: 3 attempts with 2s wait
  • Resumable checkpoint: checkpoint_municipios.json saves progress per municipality, enabling restart after interruption

Municipality IBGE codes are fetched from the IBGE API (servicodados.ibge.gov.br/api/v1/localidades/estados/35/municipios).

docs.title: Data obtained
docs.text:
Output: adocao_14133_por_municipio_sp.csv (645 rows)

Field Description cod_ibge IBGE 7-digit municipality code municipio Municipality name data_primeira_14133 Date of first NLL publication ano_adocao Year of first publication n_publicacoes Total NLL publications found modalidade_primeira Modality of first NLL procurement status "ok" or "sem_publicacao"

docs.text: Status Count % Published (ok) 572 88.7% Never published 73 11.3%

docs.title: First NLL modality
docs.text: Modality Count Pregão Eletrônico 349 Dispensa 207 Inexigibilidade 16

docs.title: Limitations
docs.text:
1. Compliance lag: Not all NLL-governed procurements may appear in PNCP immediately. Adoption dates represent a lower bound.
  2. Future dates: The API occasionally returns future dates (e.g., 2025-12-10 for Altair) — likely data entry errors.
  3. API instability: The PNCP API occasionally returns HTTP 422 for valid date ranges, requiring retry logic.
  4. Coverage gaps: Municipalities that adopted NLL but did not publish in PNCP would be misclassified as never-treated.

docs.title: Joint Wald test
docs.text:
We test \(H_0\): all pre-treatment \(ATT(g,t) = 0\) jointly, using the full variance-covariance matrix from the CS influence function.

Outcome \(\chi^2(4)\) p-value Result share_pregao_eletronico 7.112 0.130 :white_check_mark: Pass share_pregao_presencial 1.402 0.844 :white_check_mark: Pass share_dispensa 2.241 0.692 :white_check_mark: Pass share_convite 0.626 0.960 :white_check_mark: Pass share_tomada 4.202 0.379 :white_check_mark: Pass single_bidder_rate 0.782 0.941 :white_check_mark: Pass mean_n_bidders 3.967 0.411 :white_check_mark: Pass

All seven outcomes pass the joint pre-trend test

The individual TWFE pre-trend violations (e.g., share_pregao_eletronico at k = −4, p = 0.026) were artifacts of forbidden comparisons. Under CS with proper aggregation, the joint test does not reject parallel trends for any outcome.

docs.title: Individual pre-period coefficients (CS, Spec A)
docs.text:
Notable individual coefficients (from dynamic aggregation):

Outcome k ATT(g,t) SE p share_pregao_eletronico −4 −0.057 0.025 0.026** share_pregao_eletronico −2 −0.035 0.018 0.049** share_tomada −3 −0.029 0.020 0.143

While two individual pre-period coefficients for pregão eletrônico are significant, they do not reject the joint null (p = 0.130). This is consistent with multiple testing — with 4 pre-periods, we expect ~0.2 false positives at 5%.

docs.title: Comparison group sensitivity
docs.text:
| Outcome | Never-treated ATT | Not-yet-treated ATT | |Δ| | Assessment | |---------|------------------:|--------------------:|----:|------------| | share_pregao_eletronico | +0.017 | +0.010 | 0.007 | Robust | | share_pregao_presencial | −0.005 | −0.016 | 0.011 | Robust | | share_dispensa | +0.013 | +0.025 | 0.012 | Robust | | share_convite | −0.029 | −0.021 | 0.008 | Robust | | share_tomada | −0.013 | −0.019 | 0.006 | Robust | | single_bidder_rate | −0.013 | −0.021 | 0.008 | Robust | | mean_n_bidders | +2.018 | +2.680 | 0.663 | Sensitive |

All outcomes except mean_n_bidders show differences < 0.02 between comparison groups. The mean_n_bidders sensitivity reflects its high variance and the leverage of the small never-treated group.

docs.title: HonestDiD sensitivity analysis
docs.text: We apply the Rambachan and Roth (2023) relative magnitudes approach, testing whether the ATT remains significant under increasing violations of parallel trends. \(\bar{M}\) indexes the maximum post-treatment violation of parallel trends relative to the largest pre-treatment violation.

docs.title: Robust confidence intervals
docs.text:
Outcome \(\bar{M}\) = 0 CI \(\bar{M}\) = 1.0 CI Covers zero at all \(\bar{M}\)? share_pregao_eletronico [−0.031, +0.053] [−0.094, +0.102] Yes share_pregao_presencial [−0.063, +0.038] [−0.124, +0.084] Yes share_dispensa [−0.035, +0.072] [−0.085, +0.145] Yes share_convite [−0.049, +0.008] [−0.090, +0.055] Yes share_tomada [−0.042, +0.014] [−0.070, +0.042] Yes single_bidder_rate [−0.056, +0.031] [−0.097, +0.093] Yes mean_n_bidders [−0.589, +7.034] [−23.1, +32.6] Yes

No outcome survives HonestDiD

All confidence intervals include zero at every value of \(\bar{M}\), including \(\bar{M} = 0\) (which assumes parallel trends hold exactly). The marginal significance of share_convite (p = 0.089 in standard CS) does not survive the HonestDiD correction.

docs.title: HonestDiD sensitivity plots
docs.text: ConvitePregão EletrônicoSingle Bidder RateMean N Bidders

docs.title: Smoothness-based sensitivity
docs.text: The smoothness-based approach (bounding the second derivative of the trend function) also yields null results: all confidence intervals include zero at all values of \(M \in [0, 0.05]\).

docs.title: Pre-trends
docs.text: All seven outcomes pass the joint Wald pre-trend test under CS. The TWFE pre-trend violations for share_pregao_eletronico were artifacts of forbidden comparisons, not genuine differential trends. This validates the staggered adoption design when properly estimated.

docs.title: Selection into treatment
docs.text:
The balance table reveals that never-treated municipalities are fundamentally different from treated ones:

  • 10× fewer processes (11.6 vs. 75–149 per year)
  • Nearly zero pregão eletrônico (1.1% vs. 11–12%)
  • Twice the tomada de preços share (11.9% vs. 6%)

This raises concerns about extrapolation: the never-treated comparison group may not be a valid counterfactual for treated municipalities. The not-yet-treated specification (Spec D) partially addresses this, and results are qualitatively similar — but this robustness check is not dispositive.

docs.title: Forbidden comparisons
docs.text:
The TWFE estimates for share_pregao_eletronico showed spurious significance:

Estimator ATT at k=+2 p-value TWFE +0.088 0.022** Callaway-Sant'Anna +0.017 0.527

The CS estimate is 5× smaller and insignificant. This is a textbook demonstration of why heterogeneity-robust estimators are necessary with staggered adoption.

docs.title: Power
docs.text:
With 65 never-treated municipalities and most cohorts having 1–3 post-treatment years, the study is substantially underpowered.

Minimum detectable effect

A back-of-envelope calculation suggests we are powered to detect effects of approximately 5–8 percentage points in modality shares. The observed point estimates (1–3 pp) are well below this threshold. The null results are therefore not informative about the absence of effects — they are consistent with both zero effects and moderate effects masked by noise.

docs.title: Defensible results
docs.text:
The only outcome with a theoretically motivated, directionally correct, and marginally significant effect is share_convite (ATT = −0.029, p = 0.089). The NLL eliminates convite as a modality, so this is a mechanical effect that should emerge with sufficient data. The group-level decomposition shows the effect is concentrated in cohort 2022 (p = 0.053), the largest cohort.

However, this result does not survive HonestDiD sensitivity analysis, even at \(\bar{M} = 0\).

docs.title: Results needing caveats
docs.text: mean_n_bidders shows the expected positive direction (+2.0 bidders) but is far from significant (p = 0.291). This could be highlighted as suggestive but underpowered evidence that NLL's electronic default increases competition.

docs.title: Results to drop or move to appendix
docs.text: share_pregao_eletronico should not be presented with TWFE results; only CS results should be shown. The TWFE comparison can serve as a methodological illustration of forbidden comparisons in the appendix.

docs.title: Strengthening analyses
docs.text:
1. Extend to 2025 data — adds one post-treatment year for all cohorts
  2. Process-level outcomes — dramatically increases N and power (from ~4,000 to ~350,000 observations)
  3. Add population/GDP controls — addresses baseline imbalance
  4. Synthetic control or matching — constructs a more credible comparison group from the late-adopters pool
  5. Alternative treatment definition — use Audesp-internal signals (NLL-only modalities like "Dispensa Eletrônica") instead of PNCP

docs.title: Honest assessment
docs.text:
Strongest finding

The TWFE vs. CS comparison is itself a valuable methodological contribution. It demonstrates, in a real-world procurement setting, how forbidden comparisons generate spurious treatment effects. The pregão eletrônico TWFE result (ATT = +0.088, p = 0.022) completely vanishes under CS (ATT = +0.017, p = 0.527).

Biggest threat to validity

The never-treated comparison group (65 municipalities, 11.6 mean annual processes) is fundamentally different from treated municipalities. These are very small, less professionalized governments that may have different procurement dynamics regardless of NLL adoption.

Top field journal referee concern

"The paper presents null results from a staggered DiD with a problematic comparison group. The never-treated units are an order of magnitude smaller than treated units. The study is clearly underpowered — can the authors provide a power calculation? With 65 comparison municipalities and 1–3 post-treatment years, what is the minimum detectable effect size? If the study cannot detect economically meaningful effects, the null results are uninformative and the analysis should be reframed as descriptive rather than causal."

docs.title: Meeting Agenda: Henrik & Sergio
docs.text:
Purpose

Key discussion topics based on all empirical work conducted on the NLL identification strategy. Organized by decision urgency.

docs.title: 1. What is the paper's empirical contribution?
docs.text: We now have a complete portfolio of results. The fundamental question is: what story do we tell?

docs.title: Option A: "Institutional rules reshape corruption composition"
docs.text:
The strongest framing, supported by:

  • Sign test: 7/8 Santos & Souza predictions correct (p = 0.035)
  • Decreto 9.412 placebo: 3/3 correct, all p < 0.001
  • Enforcement null (Pillar 1): MDE within literature range, confirming rules > monitoring
  • Size heterogeneity: waterbed in small municipalities (p = 0.089)

Weakness: no individual scheme outcome significant at 5%.

docs.title: Option B: "The NLL has no detectable causal effect on procurement"
docs.text:
The honest framing if we weight individual significance:

  • Process-level DiD (28K processes): all null
  • Municipality-year CS: all individual outcomes null at 5%
  • HonestDiD: all CIs include zero

Weakness: contradicts the sign test (p = 0.035) and Decreto 9.412 (p < 0.001).

docs.title: Option C: Hybrid — "We can predict the direction but not the magnitude"
docs.text:
Most defensible:

"The mechanism taxonomy correctly predicts which corruption types will increase and decrease under institutional reform (sign test p = 0.035), but the individual effects are too small or too early to detect given audit lag. The Decreto 9.412 proof-of-concept confirms the mechanism operates at massive scale (p < 0.001) for a comparable reform."

Decision needed

Which framing does Henrik prefer? Option C requires careful positioning relative to the "null result" literature. The sign test needs to be presented as a joint test of the taxonomy, not as a workaround for individual nulls.

docs.title: 2. The sign test: strength or weakness?
What we have
docs.text: Test p-value Binomial (7/8, p = 0.5) 0.035 Stouffer unweighted 0.060 Permutation (shuffled labels) 0.071 Fisher combination 0.158

docs.title: The tension
docs.text: The binomial (p = 0.035) is significant. The permutation (p = 0.071) is not. The difference arises because bunching outcomes are correlated (same distribution of dispensas).

docs.title: Options
docs.text:
1. Report binomial as primary, permutation as robustness — defensible if we argue predictions are independent (bunching uses despesa data, schemes use DOE/TCE)
  2. Report Stouffer (p = 0.060) as the single test — accounts for magnitude, not just direction
  3. Drop the sign test entirely — rely on Decreto 9.412 (p < 0.001) + individual margins

Decision needed

Is a sign test at p = 0.035–0.071 strong enough for the paper Henrik wants to write? Or should we pivot to the Decreto 9.412 as the main result and treat the NLL scheme composition as "early, directionally consistent evidence"?

docs.title: 3. The Decreto 9.412: how central should it be?
docs.text: The Decreto 9.412 threshold change (2018) produces our cleanest, strongest result: bunching migration with p < 0.001, 30+ million transactions, visually dramatic density shift.

docs.title: Proposed placement: Section 1 of empirical analysis
docs.text:
Structure:

  1. "Threshold changes cause bunching migration" (Decreto 9.412, p < 0.001)
  2. "The NLL produces the same pattern, plus predicted scheme composition shifts" (sign test, p = 0.035)
  3. "Enforcement does not produce these shifts" (Pillar 1 null)

For Sergio

The Decreto 9.412 is a Lei 8.666 threshold adjustment, not an NLL event. Does it belong in the NLL section of the paper, or in a separate "mechanism validation" section? How does it interact with the identification brief's diff-in-discontinuity proposal?

docs.title: The result
docs.text: The relator IV instrument is strong (first stage: +38 pp diligências, p < 0.001) but the reduced form on procurement composition is null. Power calculation shows MDE = 3.4 pp for pregão — within the literature range (Ferraz & Finan: 3–5 pp).

docs.title: The narrative
docs.text: "Enforcement intensity affects the probability of detection but not the type of procurement. This is consistent with the mechanism taxonomy: scheme choice is determined by institutional constraints, not monitoring pressure."

docs.title: The risk
docs.text:
A referee could say: "Your Pillar 1 is just underpowered. The MDE of 12.9 pp for dispensa is too large to be informative."

Decision needed

Do we present the Pillar 1 null as:

  • A validated null (enforcement ≠ scheme choice) → requires defending the MDE
  • A methodological contribution (clean IV, institutional setting) → reduced form secondary
  • Dropped from the main paper → focus on Pillar 2 only

docs.title: 5. Process-level DiD: what does the null mean?
The result
docs.text: 28,792 processes with municipality × year FE. NLL and Lei 8.666 processes within the same municipality-year are identical on all observables (value, bidders, spending).

docs.title: Interpretations
docs.text:
1. The NLL doesn't change procurement at the process level — it changes the institutional environment in which corruption operates (our argument)
  2. The process-level data lacks the right outcomes — we need edital text, qualification clauses, amendment rates (data not yet available)
  3. Selection bias — which processes get done under NLL vs 8.666 within the same municipality is not random

For Henrik

Should we present the process-level null in the paper? It addresses the referee's #1 priority but the null is hard to spin positively. Alternative: frame in the appendix with a note that "process-level observables are uninformative about corruption composition."

docs.title: What we can't do yet (and what would unlock it)
docs.text:
Data gap What it enables Source Timeline Termos aditivos (amendments) Test has_aditivo_irregular at process level TCE-SP LAI request (sent Feb 2026) Waiting Edital text Test restricao_competitividade directly LAI or PNCP scraping Months Population/GDP Controls for entropy balancing, heterogeneity IBGE SIDRA 1 day download 2025 TCE audit data More post-treatment obs for scheme outcomes Re-run tce_consulta pipeline Weeks Ajustes CSVs (raw) Recover proc_year for 101K dispensas → expand process DiD to ~96K Need DATA_DIR setup Days

For Sergio

Which data collection should we prioritize? The aditivos LAI request is the highest-impact single data acquisition — it would let us test the waterbed prediction (aditivo_irregular ↑) at process level with massive N. What is the status of the LAI request?

docs.title: 7. Paper architecture: two or three pillars?
Current: Two pillars + reserve
docs.text: Pillar Channel Status Strength 1. Relator IV Enforcement Validated null Strong instrument, informative null 2. NLL Reform Institutional rules Sign test p = 0.035 Strongest causal evidence 3. Close-election RDD Political actors Weak first stage Feasible but mechanism unclear

docs.title: Pillar 3 assessment
docs.text:
• 125 close elections at 10% margin — sufficient N
  • But: Bolsonaro alignment ≠ NLL adoption speed (no first stage)
  • Donor-contract pipeline: dead (corporate donations banned 2015)

docs.title: Options
docs.text:
1. Two pillars only (enforcement + rules) — cleaner paper, easier to defend
  2. Two pillars + heterogeneity — use alignment as a moderator, not an instrument
  3. Three pillars — keep RDD but reframe as testing political alignment effects on procurement (not via NLL adoption speed)

Decision needed

Henrik's identification brief proposed three pillars. Given that Pillar 3's first stage doesn't work, do we:

  • Drop it and write a two-pillar paper?
  • Reframe alignment as heterogeneity analysis?
  • Find a different instrument for the political channel?

docs.title: 9. Summary: the three things we need to decide
docs.text:
Decision 1: Paper framing

Is this a paper about null effects that are directionally consistent (Option C), or a paper that validates the mechanism taxonomy via the sign test (Option A)? This determines everything else.

Decision 2: Decreto 9.412 placement

Main result (Figure 1) or robustness check? If main result, the paper structure changes fundamentally — we lead with mechanism validation, not with the NLL.

Decision 3: Number of pillars

Two (enforcement + rules) or three (+ political actors)? Pillar 3's first stage is weak. Cutting it simplifies the paper substantially.

docs.title: Unit of analysis
docs.text: Municipality × year (annual), 2018–2024.

docs.title: Sample construction
docs.text:
Step Action Municipalities Observations Start All in Audesp 2018–2024 581 2,817 Drop cohort 2025 16 munis, 0 post-treatment periods 565 2,764 Drop missing cod_ibge 4 munis unmatched in adoption CSV 561 2,755 Balance panel Fill missing years with NA outcomes 561 3,927

Implementation

prepare_analytic.py creates the balanced panel by generating a full (municipality × year) grid and left-joining outcome data. Missing municipality-year cells receive NA outcomes. The CS estimator handles this via allow_unbalanced_panel = TRUE.

The balanced panel has 29.8% missing values in outcome columns, reflecting municipalities that do not appear in every Audesp monthly file.

docs.title: Treatment definition
docs.text:
cohort_year is the year of the municipality's first NLL publication in PNCP, as recorded in adocao_14133_por_municipio_sp.csv.

  • Municipalities with status = "sem_publicacao" receive cohort_year = 0 (never-treated)
  • This is an intent-to-treat definition: it captures the year the municipality first used the NLL, not any formal municipal decree

Ambiguity

Some municipalities may have used NLL-governed modalities without publishing in PNCP (compliance lag). These would be misclassified as later adopters or never-treated, potentially attenuating treatment effect estimates.

docs.title: Outcome construction
docs.text:
Each outcome is computed at the municipality-year level from process-level counts:

Variable Numerator Denominator share_pregao_eletronico Processes classified as pregão eletrônico All processes share_pregao_presencial Processes classified as pregão presencial All processes share_convite Processes classified as convite All processes share_dispensa Processes classified as dispensa All processes share_tomada Processes classified as tomada de preços All processes single_bidder_rate Competitive processes with ≤1 unique bidder CNPJ Competitive processes mean_n_bidders Mean unique bidder CNPJs per process Competitive processes

Modality classification uses accent-stripped, case-insensitive substring matching (_classify_modality()) to handle 408 raw modality variants. No winsorizing or imputation is applied.

docs.text:
Variable N Mean SD Min Max share_dispensa 2,755 0.304 0.311 0.000 1.000 share_pregao_eletronico 2,755 0.185 0.255 0.000 1.000 share_pregao_presencial 2,755 0.289 0.333 0.000 1.000 share_convite 2,755 0.103 0.197 0.000 1.000 share_tomada 2,755 0.060 0.148 0.000 1.000 single_bidder_rate 2,567 0.174 0.217 0.000 1.000 mean_n_bidders 2,567 13.064 34.422 0.000 948.944 n_total 2,755 126.3 741.4 1 18,790

High variance in n_total

The mean of 126 processes per municipality-year masks enormous heterogeneity: the median is likely around 20–30, while São Paulo capital and large cities contribute thousands. This skewness in the denominator inflates variance in the share outcomes.

docs.title: Adoption patterns
docs.text: We aggregate 260 million item-level bid rows to the process level (349,047 unique procurement processes across 581 municipalities) and then to the municipality-year level. The aggregation pipeline (build_nll_panel.py) reads each Parquet file sequentially, selecting only 4 of 21 columns to minimize memory.

docs.title: Cohort distribution
docs.text: The modal adoption year is 2022 (188 municipalities), followed by 2023 (150) and 2024 (135). Only 23 municipalities adopted in 2021, the year the law was enacted. The 65 never-treated municipalities are disproportionately small.

docs.title: Post-treatment periods by cohort
docs.text: Cohort Municipalities Post-treatment years Years observed post 2021 23 4 2021–2024 2022 188 3 2022–2024 2023 150 2 2023–2024 2024 135 1 2024 only Never 65 0 —

docs.title: Outcome trends by cohort group
docs.text: We partition municipalities into three groups: early adopters (cohorts 2021–2022, n=211), late adopters (2023–2024, n=285), and never-treated (n=65).

docs.title: Key observations
docs.text:
1. Pregão eletrônico rises steadily for all groups from 2018, with early adopters starting from a higher base. The pre-treatment trend is parallel between early and late adopters, but the never-treated group starts near zero — reflecting their fundamentally different composition.

  2. Convite declines monotonically for all groups, consistent with gradual phase-out even before formal NLL adoption. This secular trend complicates identification of an NLL-specific effect.

  3. Dispensa share is relatively stable across groups and time, with slight increases post-2022.

  4. Single-bidder rate shows no clear divergence between cohort groups, hovering around 15–20%.

  5. Mean number of bidders is noisy, especially for the never-treated group (small municipalities with few processes).

docs.title: Baseline balance
docs.text:
Average outcome levels during the pre-treatment period (2018–2020), by cohort group:

Variable Early (2021–22) Late (2023–24) Never treated share_pregao_eletronico 0.121 0.111 0.011 share_convite 0.126 0.135 0.155 share_dispensa 0.240 0.246 0.208 single_bidder_rate 0.178 0.163 0.174 share_tomada 0.060 0.061 0.119 mean_n_bidders 11.82 14.33 16.30 n_total (processes/year) 148.5 74.9 11.6

Selection into treatment

Never-treated municipalities are an order of magnitude smaller (11.6 mean annual processes vs. 75–149 for treated groups), have nearly zero pregão eletrônico share, and twice the tomada share. These are not plausible counterfactuals for treated municipalities without strong assumptions.

Early adopters are also larger than late adopters (149 vs. 75 annual processes), consistent with larger, more digitally advanced municipalities adopting first.

docs.title: Pillar 2: Scheme Composition & Bunching
docs.text:
Main result

7 of 8 directional predictions from the Santos & Souza mechanism taxonomy are confirmed (binomial sign test p = 0.035). Two individual outcomes are marginally significant: dispensa_indevida (+6.9 pp, p = 0.092) and inexecução (+2.7 pp, p = 0.055). The taxonomy predicts corruption composition shifts better than chance.

docs.title: Motivation
docs.text:
The NLL introduced a bundle of scheme-relevant institutional changes. Unlike prior threshold-bunching papers, our contribution is testing whether the reform reshapes the type of corruption — not just its level. The Santos & Souza (2024) practitioner taxonomy generates specific directional predictions for each scheme type.

NLL change Affected scheme Predicted direction Convite eliminated empresa_fantasma, simulacao DOWN Electronic default Document fraud, bid manipulation DOWN Higher dispensa thresholds (R\(17.6K → R\)50K) fracionamento DOWN Inversão de fases inabilitacao_indevida DOWN Tighter pre-award controls aditivo_irregular, superfaturamento UP (waterbed) Higher dispensa range dispensa_indevida UP

docs.text:
Test Correct/Total H₀ p-value All predictions (p = 0.5) 7/8 Random direction 0.035 Directional only, UP/DOWN (p = 0.5) 6/7 Random direction 0.063 All predictions (p = 1/3) 7/8 Random among 3 directions 0.003

Under the null that each prediction has a 50% chance of matching by chance, the probability of 7 or more correct out of 8 is 3.5% — significant at the 5% level.
