docs.title: Bunching time series
docs.text: The descriptive time series shows bunching at the old goods threshold (R\(17,600, red) rising through 2023 then **declining in 2024**, while bunching at the new threshold (R\)50,000, blue) begins to rise — consistent with threshold migration.

docs.title: Panel B: Individually significant supporting results
docs.text: Two outcomes achieve marginal statistical significance on their own, corroborating the taxonomy's predictions.

docs.title: Dispensa indevida (+6.9 pp, p = 0.092)
docs.text:
Prediction: UP

NLL raised dispensa thresholds from R\(17,600 to R\)50,000 (goods) and R\(33,000 to R\)100,000 (works). This expands the range where sole-source procurement is legal, creating more opportunities for improper use of the dispensa modality.

Spec ATT SE p Source D (not-yet-treated) +0.069 0.041 0.092* LLM-extracted tipos

Group-level effects: cohort 2022 (+8.9 pp, p = 0.122), cohort 2023 (+11.7 pp, p = 0.171). The effect grows for later cohorts, consistent with increasing NLL compliance.

docs.title: Inexecução (+2.7 pp, p = 0.055)
docs.text:
Interpretation: transition costs

Not predicted by the taxonomy (classified as STABLE), but interpretable as transition friction: municipalities adapting to new NLL procedures experience more findings of contract non-performance during the adjustment period.

Spec ATT SE p Source D (not-yet-treated) +0.027 0.014 0.055* DOE panel (incl cohort 2024)

Group-level effects: cohort 2022 (+3.1 pp, p = 0.088*). Pre-trend violation flagged — interpret with caution.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
For the paper

Main result: The Santos & Souza mechanism taxonomy correctly predicts the direction of 7 out of 8 corruption composition shifts induced by NLL adoption (sign test p = 0.035). No individual outcome is significant at 5%, reflecting audit lag and limited post-treatment data. Two outcomes — dispensa_indevida (p = 0.092) and inexecução (p = 0.055) — approach significance individually and corroborate the mechanism-level narrative.

Interpretation: The NLL reshapes the form of corruption, not just its level. Schemes that depend on eliminated modalities (fracionamento, simulação) decline, while schemes that exploit expanded dispensa authority (dispensa indevida) and post-award extraction margins (aditivo irregular) increase. The single incorrect prediction (sobrepreço) has a near-zero point estimate, consistent with noise rather than a wrong prediction.

Limitation: Individual effects are underpowered due to TCE audit lag (1–3 years), small comparison group (65–73 never-treated), and limited post-treatment observations. Power will improve substantially as 2024–2025 audit data become available.

docs.title: M1. The Relator IV Does Not Yet Deliver a Causal Result
docs.text:
Problem. The relator IV is the paper's most developed identification strategy. The instrument --- quasi-random rotation of TCE-SP rapporteurs across municipalities --- is institutionally compelling and passes standard balance tests (joint Fisher p = 0.979, Cramer's V for UR x relator = 0.05). The mechanism chain works: tough relators demand more diligencias (+38pp, p < 0.001) and restitution (+14pp, p = 0.001). But the reduced-form results on procurement behavior are null or marginal. Share of dispensa, share of pregao, and payee HHI are precisely zero at N ~ 8,000. The only responsive outcome --- share of contracts with positive overruns --- is marginal (beta = -0.860, p = 0.062) at N = 582.

Why it matters. A validated instrument with a strong mechanism chain but no detectable behavioral response is an institutional null result, not a causal anchor for the paper. The enforcement cascade finding (tough contas relator at t -> more non-contas irregularity decisions at t + 2, p = 0.032) is interesting but is about the auditing system's internal dynamics, not about corruption behavior.

Implicit F-statistic

The type-specific first stage for fracionamento (beta = -0.164, p = 0.071) implies t ~ 1.81, hence F ~ 3.3. This is well below the Stock-Yogo threshold of 10 and below the Lee et al. (2022, AER) threshold of 4. For direcionamento (p = 0.191), F ~ 1.7. These are weak first stages by any standard.

The LATE problem. Even if the first stage were strong, the LATE identifies the effect of auditor toughness on procurement behavior --- the marginal municipality that changes behavior in response to drawing a tougher auditor. This is an interesting parameter for enforcement, but it is not the parameter the paper's 12 hypotheses are about. The hypotheses concern how market structure, contract complexity, and monitoring technology determine which type of corruption arises.

Path forward.

  1. The newly added behavioral outcomes (Q4 spending concentration, repeat-contractor share) that exploit the full despesa panel (N ~ 8,000 rather than 582) should be reported. If Q4 share or repeat-contractor share moves, this substantially strengthens the result.
  2. The predicted irregularity index approach (training ML models on objective procurement observables, then using predicted scores as auditor-independent outcomes) is the right idea for resolving the detection confound. Implement it.
  3. Be explicit that the relator IV tests enforcement response, not scheme choice, and position it accordingly.

docs.title: M2. The NLL Reform Test Is the Missing Empirical Spine
docs.text:
Problem. The Lei 14.133/2021 staggered adoption test is the more promising identification strategy for the paper's core question --- it directly tests whether changing institutional rules reshapes the composition of corruption schemes as the taxonomy predicts. The directional predictions are specific (simulacao down, emergencia fabricada up, aditivo irregular up) and uniquely testable with the LLM-extracted codebook. Yet this test has not been implemented. There is no script, no estimation, no result.

Why it matters. For a top-5, the question is not whether the data exist or the design is plausible --- it is whether the paper shows results. The NLL test is the natural complement to the relator IV: the IV tests enforcement intensity (holding rules constant), while the NLL tests rule changes (holding enforcement constant). Together, they form the empirical spine the paper claims but does not yet have.

Status

Without the NLL results, the paper has one marginal causal result (overruns, p = 0.062) and extensive descriptive evidence.

Path forward. This is the single highest-return investment the authors can make. Build the municipal adoption panel from fundamento_dispensa, estimate the staggered DiD on irregularity tipo shares, and report the results. Even null results would be informative.

docs.title: M3. LLM Classification as Dependent Variable: Measurement Error
docs.text:
Problem. The paper uses GPT-4o-mini to extract structured irregularity classifications from TCE-SP relatorio e voto documents, then uses these classifications as outcome variables in regressions and as the basis for the scheme typology. Section 6.3 describes a validation plan (inter-rater reliability, prompt robustness, external consistency) but the results are marked "TODO."

The 72.4% sustained rate among extracted allegations means 27.6% of allegations are not upheld by the tribunal --- and the paper does not discuss whether misclassification is classical (attenuating) or directional.

Why it matters. If the LLM systematically over-detects direcionamento relative to conluio (plausible --- specification rigging is described in more detail in relatorio text than implicit collusion), then the composition of the scheme distribution is biased. The Spearman rho = 0.77 between DOE keyword detection and LLM extraction is reassuring for rank ordering, but does not validate the fine-grained 35-type classification. The gap between 6 keyword categories (where rho = 0.77) and 35 fine-grained types (where no validation exists) is large.

Path forward.

  1. Complete the validation exercise. At minimum: code 50--100 allegations manually and compute Cohen's kappa at the fine-grained type level.
  2. Conduct sensitivity analysis restricting to sustained allegations only.
  3. For compositional outcomes in the NLL DiD, use the coarser 6-type categorization (where cross-source validation exists) as primary specification.

docs.title: M4. The 20.7% Linkage Rate and the 56-Case Tier 4
docs.text:
Problem. Of 19,378 judicial cases, 4,015 (20.7%) are linked to procurement records by any method, and only 56 have full bid-level detail. The paper's sharpest tests --- bidding signatures, price-amendment profiles, composite red-flag validation --- all depend on this thin sample.

Selection concern. Cases that can be linked are not a random subsample. Process-number linking mechanically favors recent, formally competed procurements. Firm-name linking favors cases where firms (not just individuals) are prosecuted --- only 29% of improbidade cases name firm defendants. The 80% without firm defendants are more likely to involve Type A (bypass) corruption, which means the linked sample likely under-represents bypass corruption.

The bidding signature analysis (Section 8.2) actually uses 12,432 DOE-linked processes rather than court-linked cases --- a larger sample, but a different population (audit-flagged irregularities vs. judicially revealed corruption).

Path forward.

  1. Report observable characteristics of linked vs. unlinked cases and test for balance.
  2. Implement the 4-step narrowing exercise to expand from 56 to 200+ cases.
  3. Reframe: municipality-level analyses (N = 644 municipalities) are the paper's strength. Process-level analyses (N = 56) are proof-of-concept illustrations, not tests.

docs.title: M5. Contribution Relative to Existing Literature
docs.text:
Problem. The paper positions itself as bridging four literatures that "never meet." But the gap may be narrower than claimed:

  • Conley and Decarolis (2016, JLEO) linked Italian court cases to procurement data and validated detection screens
  • Campos et al. (2021, JPE) provided mechanism-level evidence on Odebrecht
  • Carbone (2024) links judicial evidence to auction participation
  • The detection literature (Imhof 2018; Kawai and Nakabayashi 2022) has moved toward mechanism identification through structural estimation

The real contribution is narrower and more defensible: the scale of mechanism-labeled corruption (19,378 cases vs. N = 1 for Campos et al.) and the LLM-enabled classification that makes this scale feasible. This is a measurement and data contribution, not a theoretical one. The theoretical framework (5 lenses, 12 hypotheses) is a useful organizing device, but the predictions are assembled from existing theory, not derived.

Path forward. Narrow the claimed contribution to: (i) a new empirical object (mechanism-labeled corruption at scale), (ii) the two causal tests (relator IV + NLL), and (iii) the policy diagnostic (which tool for which scheme). Drop the suggestion that the theoretical framework is a contribution in itself.

docs.title: 3. Minor Comments
docs.text:
m1. Table 1 predictions are not all testable with available data. The predictions in Table 1 (Section 3.6) span all five theoretical frameworks, but several require data the paper does not have. "Relationship duration -> scheme complexity" requires observing the same firm-municipality pair across multiple procurement events with known corrupt status --- feasible only for Tier 3/4 cases (<=855). "Complicit fiscal -> measurement fraud" requires identifying which specific official is complicit --- not in the data. The paper should mark which predictions are testable now vs. aspirational.

m2. The waterbed effect (fracionamento down, direcionamento up) is not identified. Figure 3 showing the crossover of irregularity types over 2008--2023 is the paper's most visually striking result, but it is a time series, not a causal estimate. Many things changed simultaneously: pregao adoption, Audesp implementation, changing MP priorities, economic cycles. The pregao event study (Section 7.5, Figure 5) attempts municipality-level identification but is underpowered (N = 103 late adopters) and shows no sharp break. The paper should present this as "consistent with" the waterbed hypothesis, not as evidence for it.

m3. The enforcement funnel needs context. The finding that only 31.2% of sustained irregularities result in monetary penalties, with a median fine of R$7,072 (200 UFESP), is presented as a "deterrence gap." This should be contextualized against:

  • The theoretical deterrence literature (Becker 1968 predicts that expected penalty = probability x amount must exceed expected gain; compute expected gains from the data)
  • Comparative evidence on audit court sanctioning rates in other Brazilian states or other countries
  • The fact that TCE-SP fines are only one component of enforcement --- MP prosecution (leading to loss of office, 14-year political suspension) is the high-stakes channel

m4. Network analysis (Section 9) is descriptive without a counterfactual. Finding that 28% of officials appear in 2+ processos and 73 officials span multiple municipalities is informative, but absent a counterfactual (what would the distribution look like under random assignment of officials to irregular procurement?), the hub-and-spoke claim is not tested. A permutation test comparing observed degree distributions to simulated random networks of equal size and density would add rigor. As written, Section 9 reads as case-study evidence.

m5. The coauthor's frequent-loser paper creates a tension. Genicolo-Martins and Azevedo (2026) is cited extensively and their FL screen is integrated into the empirical strategy (Sections 7.1, 7.3). But the FL paper uses BEC (state electronic procurement, 2009--2019) while this paper uses Audesp (municipal procurement, 2018--2024). The CNPJ-based bridge between the two platforms is described but not validated. How many of the 2,735 FL firms from BEC actually appear in Audesp? If few, the FL tests have no power. If many, this should be documented.

m6. The exclusion restriction for the relator IV has unstated threats. The paper tests UR x relator independence and lagged procurement outcomes -> toughness (balance tests). But two threats are not addressed:

  1. Political connections: If some conselheiros are co-opted (the paper documents the Robson Marinho and Eduardo Bittencourt Carvalho cases), and if co-opted conselheiros are non-randomly assigned to politically connected municipalities despite the rotation rule, the exclusion restriction fails. Test whether municipalities with politically connected mayors draw systematically different relators.
  2. Serial correlation in assignment: The rotation rule prevents the same relator in consecutive years, but does it prevent similar relators (e.g., all tough or all lenient)? If relator assignment exhibits autocorrelation in type-specific toughness, the leave-one-out measure is contaminated.

m7. Missing standard errors discussion. Throughout the reduced-form results, standard errors are clustered at the municipality level. Given that the treatment (relator toughness) varies at the relator x year level, clustering at the municipality level may not be the right level. If relator assignments are correlated within a year (a tough relator's LOO toughness rises when they are assigned many irregular municipalities), then clustering at the relator or relator x year level may be more appropriate. At minimum, report results under alternative clustering.

m8. The paper is too long and tries to do too much. At 2,675 lines of LaTeX with 12 hypotheses, 6 data sections, 5 theoretical frameworks, 4 identification strategies (of which 2 are implemented), and results covering prevalence, bidding signatures, execution trajectories, enforcement outcomes, network analysis, and cross-source validation --- the paper exceeds what a reader can absorb. Much of what is currently in the main text (network descriptives, enforcement funnel, cross-validation) should move to an appendix or companion paper.

Standard top-5 advice

One or two clean results, well-identified, thoroughly discussed. Focus signals confidence.

m9. Henrik's comments are right. The two Hypothesis annotations from hsigstad (motivate with policy question, not literature gap; question relevance of Section 2.1 detection literature) align with standard editorial advice. The introduction should open with the policy problem (how to fight corruption effectively requires knowing how it is organized) before discussing what the literature misses.

m10. The conclusion and appendices are empty. Section 12 and Appendices A--B are "TODO." A paper submitted for review cannot have placeholder sections. Even for an internal draft, the conclusion should be written --- it forces clarity about what has been shown.

docs.title: Referee Report
docs.text:
Paper: "The Organization of Corruption in Public Procurement"

Authors: Sergio Galletta (ETH Zurich), Darcio Genicolo Martins (Insper), Henrik Sigstad (BI Norwegian Business School)

Journal Target: Top-5 Economics Journal (AER / QJE / JPE / REStud)

Date: March 2026

docs.title: Recommendation
docs.text:
Major Revision

The paper addresses a first-order question with a genuinely novel combination of data and methods. However, it does not yet deliver a convincing causal result. With a clean NLL compositional result, a strengthened relator IV, and completed LLM validation, this paper could be publishable in a top-5 journal. Without those, it belongs in a strong field journal.

docs.title: Report Structure
docs.text: Section Content Summary What the paper does and preliminary assessment Major Comments 5 deal-breakers that must be addressed Minor Comments 10 improvements that strengthen the paper Recommendation Formal verdict with justification Roadmap for Top-5 7 concrete actions with difficulty and impact

docs.title: Quick Reference: Key Numbers
docs.text: Metric Value Court cases 19,378 Municipalities 644 Procurement processes 325,523 Cases linked to procurement 4,015 (20.7%) Cases with bid-level detail 56 LLM-extracted irregularities 1,896 Relator IV first-stage F (fracionamento) ~3.3 Relator IV overrun p-value 0.062 NLL DiD implementation status Not started LLM validation status TODO

docs.title: 4. Recommendation
docs.text:
Major Revision

The paper addresses a first-order question with a genuinely novel combination of data and methods. The mechanism-labeled corruption dataset is a real contribution, and the theoretical framework provides useful structure.

However, the paper does not yet deliver a convincing causal result:

  • The relator IV produces mostly null reduced forms
  • The NLL reform test is unimplemented
  • The LLM validation is incomplete
  • The process-level sample (N = 56) is too thin for the sharpest tests

These are not fatal problems --- the data and institutional knowledge exist to address all of them --- but they require substantial work.

docs.title: What Would Change the Verdict
docs.text: Scenario Verdict Clean NLL compositional result + strengthened relator IV + completed LLM validation Accept at top-5 NLL results null + relator IV still marginal + LLM validated Strong field journal (JEEA, REStat, JDE) No new causal results + LLM validated + descriptives polished Mid-tier field journal Current state (no NLL, marginal IV, no validation) Reject at top-5

docs.title: The Core Issue
docs.text: The paper has more theory than identification and more data than results. The 12 hypotheses, 5 theoretical frameworks, and 14 clean parquet tables represent an extraordinary investment in infrastructure. But a top-5 publication requires converting that infrastructure into one or two well-identified, clearly stated findings. The paper is currently trying to be everything --- typology paper, causal paper, detection paper, network paper, measurement paper --- and needs to decide what it is.

docs.title: 5. Roadmap for Top-5
docs.text: Seven concrete actions, ordered by priority. Each with difficulty, expected impact, and dependencies.

docs.title: Action 1: Implement the NLL Staggered DiD
docs.text:
Priority: #1 --- Critical

What to do. Build municipal adoption panel from fundamento_dispensa (identifies old vs. new law for each dispensa). Estimate event study on irregularity tipo shares --- both the 37-type LLM codebook and the coarser 6-type keyword categories. Report pre-trends and compositional shifts. Key test: does the direction of change for each tipo match the Santos & Souza predictions?

Predicted change Tipo Direction Cover bidding simulacao :arrow_down: Shell companies in convite empresa_fantasma :arrow_down: Fracionamento at old thresholds fracionamento :arrow_down: shift :arrow_right: Specification rigging direcionamento :arrow_right: or :arrow_up: Fabricated emergencies emergencia_fabricada :arrow_up: Post-award extraction aditivo_irregular :arrow_up:
  • Difficulty: Medium (data ready, design articulated, needs coding + estimation)
  • Impact: Critical --- this is the paper's most promising test of its core claim (scheme substitution in response to institutional change)
  • Dependencies: LLM extraction Batch 1 complete; build_consulta_llm.py run to normalize

docs.title: Action 2: Report New Full-Panel Behavioral Outcomes for the Relator IV
docs.text:
Priority: #2 --- Substantial

What to do. Run the newly implemented Q4 spending concentration, repeat-contractor share, competition index, and payment gap outcomes. Report reduced-form coefficients. If any are significant at N ~ 8,000, this transforms the relator IV from "suggestive" to "credible."

Outcome Source Expected N Why promising Q4 spending share despesa ~8,000 Full panel; novel; gaming signal Repeat-contractor share despesa ~7,400 Full panel; incumbency entrenchment Competition index licitacao+bid ~538 Theory-driven composite Payment timing gap contract+empenho ~550 Execution-stage measure
  • Difficulty: Low (code already written, needs execution on data machine)
  • Impact: Substantial if any outcome moves; critical for keeping relator IV as pillar
  • Dependencies: Build outputs must be generated from raw data

docs.title: Action 3: Complete LLM Validation
docs.text:
Priority: #3 --- Critical

What to do. Manually code 100 irregularity allegations at fine-grained type level. Compute Cohen's kappa and confusion matrix against GPT-4o-mini. Test prompt robustness (3--5 prompt variants). Report results in Section 6.3.

Target metrics:

  • Cohen's kappa > 0.7 at 6-type level -> confident
  • Cohen's kappa 0.5--0.7 -> usable with caveats

  • Cohen's kappa < 0.5 -> retreat to coarser categories

  • Difficulty: Medium (requires legal expertise for coding; ~40 hours of RA time)

  • Impact: Critical --- without validation, the entire scheme classification is an assumption
  • Dependencies: None (can be done in parallel with everything else)

docs.title: Action 4: Expand Tier 4 via Name-Matching Refinement
docs.text:
Priority: #4 --- Substantial

What to do. Implement the 4-step narrowing procedure:

  1. Municipality filter (restrict firm matches to same municipality)
  2. Time window (firm active during court-referenced period)
  3. Proc-number cross-reference (match extracted proc numbers to despesa)
  4. LLM extraction from sentenca text (~1,270 API calls)

Target: Expand from 56 to 200+ process-linked cases.

  • Difficulty: Medium-high (~1,270 API calls; data engineering for steps 1--3)
  • Impact: Substantial --- makes bid-signature regressions credible rather than illustrative
  • Dependencies: Steps 1--3 are pure data operations; Step 4 requires API budget

docs.title: Action 5: Implement McCrary Bunching Test for H1
docs.text:
Priority: #5 --- Substantial

What to do. Apply Cattaneo, Jansson, and Ma (2020) local polynomial density estimator to contract-value distribution around dispensa/convite thresholds. Compare density discontinuity between fracionamento-revealed municipalities and controls.

  • Difficulty: Low (standard procedure, R package rddensity or Python equivalent; data ready in licitacao.parquet)
  • Impact: Substantial --- threshold bunching is the cleanest, most visual test of any single hypothesis; H1 is the only hypothesis where the mechanism is deterministic
  • Dependencies: None (can be run immediately)

docs.title: Action 6: Resolve Measurement Error in LLM Classifier
docs.text:
Priority: #6 --- Depends on kappa

What to do. After completing Action 3, implement either SIMEX correction (if classification error rates are estimable from the validation exercise) or a bootstrap sensitivity analysis that draws from the confusion matrix to assess how misclassification affects compositional estimates.

  • Difficulty: High (requires methodological care; no off-the-shelf solution for multinomial classification error in panel outcomes)
  • Impact: Marginal to substantial depending on error rates
  • Dependencies: Action 3 (validation results needed first)

docs.title: Action 7: Restructure the Paper
docs.text:
Priority: #7 --- Substantial for readability

What to do. Move network analysis (Section 9), enforcement funnel (Section 8.4), and cross-source validation (Section 10) to appendix. Main text structure:

  1. Introduction (policy-motivated, per Henrik's comment)
  2. Framework (5 lenses, streamlined)
  3. Data + Classification + Validation
  4. Relator IV results
  5. NLL results
  6. Discussion

Target: 40--50 pages (down from current ~80).

  • Difficulty: Low (editorial, not analytical)
  • Impact: Substantial for readability and reviewer goodwill
  • Dependencies: Actions 1--3 complete (so main results are known before restructuring)

docs.title: Summary Table
docs.text: # Action Difficulty Impact Dependencies 1 NLL Staggered DiD Medium Critical LLM Batch 1 2 Full-panel relator IV outcomes Low Substantial Data build 3 LLM validation (100 manual codes) Medium Critical None 4 Expand Tier 4 (56 -> 200+ cases) Medium-high Substantial API budget 5 McCrary bunching test (H1) Low Substantial None 6 Measurement error correction High Depends on kappa Action 3 7 Restructure paper Low Substantial Actions 1--3

docs.title: 1. Summary
docs.text:
This paper asks how corruption in public procurement is organized and whether its forms respond to economic fundamentals as theory predicts. The authors link 19,378 judicial decisions from the Sao Paulo State Court (TJSP) to the universe of municipal procurement data from TCE-SP (644 municipalities, 2008--2024), use LLM-based classification to extract scheme types from judicial narratives, and test whether market structure, contract incompleteness, and monitoring technology predict which corruption form arises. The paper proposes a three-type taxonomy (bypass, pre-award overpricing, post-award extraction) and documents that these types produce distinct but often subtle procurement signatures. Two identification strategies are proposed: quasi-random auditor rotation at TCE-SP and the staggered adoption of Brazil's 2021 procurement reform.

The question is first-order. The combination of judicial narratives, procurement microdata, and LLM classification at scale is genuinely novel. The theoretical framework is well-constructed, and the descriptive evidence --- particularly the compositional shift from fracionamento to direcionamento over 2008--2023 --- is striking.

Central Problem

In its current form, the paper is primarily a rich descriptive exercise with an identification strategy that does not yet deliver a convincing causal result. For a top-5 journal, the distance between the ambition of the theoretical framework (12 hypotheses, 5 theoretical lenses) and the ability of the empirical design to test those hypotheses is the central problem.

The paper needs either one clean causal result that anchors the taxonomy, or a frank reframing as a measurement contribution with descriptive evidence --- and even then, the validation of the LLM classifier needs to be completed.

docs.title: Referee Response — Implementation
docs.text:
Context

A hypothetical AER Referee 2 identified five priority actions for resubmission. All five have been implemented. This page documents each action with results.

docs.title: Action 1: Process-Level DiD (NLL vs Lei 8.666)
docs.text:
Referee's request

"Instead of municipality-year DiD, compare NLL processes vs. Lei 8.666 processes within the same municipality-year. This eliminates between-municipality confounding."

docs.title: Implementation
docs.text:
We identify the legal framework of each process from the fundamento_dispensa field: processes citing Lei 14.133 are NLL; those citing Lei 8.666 are the comparison. To recover process years (missing for dispensas), we combine three sources: edital dates, contract years, and empenho dates.

Year recovery:

Source Processes recovered data_edital 20,128 ano_contrato (contract) 31,167 (incremental) data_empenho (spending) additional Total with year 95,031 identified processes

Sample: 37,444 processes in 278 municipality-years where both NLL and Lei 8.666 coexist, across 166 municipalities.

docs.title: Specification
docs.text:
\[y_{ijt} = \alpha_{jt} + \beta \cdot \text{is\_nll}_{ijt} + \varepsilon_{ijt}\]

Municipality × year FE (\(\alpha_{jt}\)) absorbs all municipality-level confounding. Standard errors clustered at municipality level.

docs.title: Results
docs.text:
Outcome β (NLL − 8.666) SE p N Log(contract value) −0.025 0.096 0.798 28,792 Number of bidders +1.043 0.997 0.297 6,650 Single bidder (=1) −0.000 0.016 0.979 6,650 Log(total spending) +0.096 0.135 0.479 31,870

Interpretation

Even with 28,792 processes, no outcome is significant. Within the same municipality-year, NLL and Lei 8.666 processes look identical on observable outcomes. This is an informative null: the NLL changes the composition of corruption schemes (sign test p = 0.035), not process-level observables. The contribution is about reorganization of corruption, not procurement efficiency.

docs.title: Action 2: Decreto 9.412 as Main Result
docs.text:
Strongest result in the paper

Bunching migration after the 2018 threshold change: 3/3 predictions correct, all p < 0.001.

docs.title: Framing
docs.text: The Decreto 9.412 (June 2018) raised Lei 8.666 dispensa thresholds: goods R\(15,000 → R\)17,600; works R\(30,000 → R\)33,000. We use this as a proof of concept: if threshold changes cause bunching migration, the same mechanism should operate under the NLL.

docs.title: Results
docs.text:
Threshold Δ Excess mass p Prediction Match New goods R$17,600 +0.580 < 0.001 UP :white_check_mark: Old works R$30,000 −0.528 < 0.001 DOWN :white_check_mark: New works R$33,000 +0.672 < 0.001 UP :white_check_mark:

The density shift is visually dramatic: the pre-Decreto spike at R\(15,000 disappears entirely, replaced by a new spike at R\)17,600.

docs.title: Paper structure
docs.text: This result should be Figure 1: "We first establish that threshold changes cause bunching migration (Decreto 9.412), then test whether the NLL produces the same pattern."

docs.title: Action 3: Size Heterogeneity
docs.text:
Finding

The waterbed effect (has_aditivo_irregular ↑) concentrates in the smallest municipalities (Q1: +6.8 pp, p = 0.089*).

docs.title: Implementation
docs.text: Municipalities split into quintiles by pre-treatment DOE decision count. CS estimated separately for each quintile.

docs.title: Results
docs.text:
Outcome Q1 (smallest) Q2 Q3 Q4 Q5 (largest) has_aditivo_irregular +0.068* −0.001 −0.032 +0.041 +0.102 has_fracionamento +0.005 +0.026 −0.007 +0.014 −0.057 has_direcionamento −0.015 −0.008 −0.004 −0.026 −0.046 Aditivo Irregular by QuintileFracionamento by Quintile

Interpretation: Small municipalities (Q1) show the waterbed effect — tighter NLL pre-award controls push extraction to post-award contract amendments. They lack the institutional capacity for contract oversight. Large municipalities (Q5) show the largest fracionamento decline (−5.7 pp), consistent with their ability to consolidate procurement under higher thresholds.

docs.title: Formal contrast
docs.text:
Channel Procurement composition Scheme composition p Pillar 1 (Enforcement) ALL null Not tested at scheme level All p > 0.06 Pillar 2 (Institutional rules) 7/8 directions correct dispensa_indevida ↑ 0.035

Power comparison:

Outcome Pillar 1 MDE (80%) Pillar 2 ATT Detectable? share_pregao 3.4 pp — Yes (if effect existed) share_dispensa 12.9 pp — Yes (if > 13 pp)

Conclusion: Enforcement intensity (Pillar 1) does not reshape procurement composition, even though the instrument is strong (first stage: +38 pp diligências) and the MDE is within the literature range. Institutional rules (Pillar 2) do reshape composition, producing 7/8 correct directional predictions. This validates the paper's core claim: scheme choice is determined by institutional rules, not by monitoring pressure.

docs.title: Balance before reweighting
docs.text:
Variable Treated Never-treated Std. Diff n_total 90.6 9.8 +0.18 share_pregao_eletronico 0.110 0.011 +0.55 share_convite 0.142 0.203 −0.28 share_dispensa 0.191 0.126 +0.33 single_bidder_rate 0.163 0.164 −0.01

Entropy balancing failed

The optimization reached a degenerate solution: all control weights collapsed to zero. The never-treated municipalities are too fundamentally different from treated ones for entropy balancing to find valid weights. This confirms the comparison group problem.

docs.title: Implication
docs.text: The entropy balancing failure is itself informative — it proves the never-treated group is inadequate as a standalone counterfactual. The preferred specification is Spec D (not-yet-treated), where treated municipalities serve as their own controls before adoption. Results are robust across Spec A (never-treated), Spec D, and trimmed comparison groups.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
Action Result For the paper 1. Process DiD Null (p > 0.29 for all outcomes) Appendix: informative null 2. Decreto 9.412 3/3 correct, p < 0.001 Main text: Figure 1 3. Size heterogeneity Waterbed in Q1 (p = 0.089*) Main text: discussion 4. Pilar 1 vs 2 Enforcement null vs Rules 7/8 Main text: core contrast 5. Entropy balancing Failed (degenerate) Main text: transparency

Bottom line for resubmission

The process-level DiD (Action 1) does not produce the "clean causal effect" the referee wanted — NLL processes look identical to 8.666 processes on observables. But this is the finding: the NLL doesn't change process-level efficiency; it changes the composition of corruption at the institutional level. The Decreto 9.412 proof-of-concept (Action 2, p < 0.001), the sign test (7/8, p = 0.035), and the size heterogeneity (Action 3) together make the case that institutional rules reshape corruption form, not level.

docs.title: Referee Suggestions — Implementation & Results
docs.text:
Context

Eight suggestions were formulated as if from an AER referee, then implemented. This page documents each suggestion, its implementation, and the results obtained.

docs.title: Summary of all suggestions
docs.text: # Suggestion Impact Result Status 1 Joint hypothesis (Stouffer/Fisher) Very high Z = 1.56, p = 0.060 :material-check: Done 2 Transaction-level bunching (Chetty/Kleven) Very high NLL: 2/4 match; massive N :material-check: Done 3 Placebo Decreto 9.412/2018 High 3/3 correct, all p < 0.001 :material-check: Done 4 Weighted sign test (meta-analytic) Med-high Fisher p = 0.158; Stouffer p = 0.060 :material-check: Done 5 Pre-registration defense table Critical All predictions from S&S (2024) :material-check: Done 6 Intensive margin outcomes Medium Null — counts too sparse :material-check: Done 7 Complier analysis (size heterogeneity) Medium Aditivo in small munis (+5.4 pp) :material-check: Done 8 Comparison group defense Medium Balance documented; ATTs stable :material-check: Done

docs.title: Suggestion 1: Joint hypothesis test
docs.text:
Problem

The binomial sign test assumes independence. Four bunching outcomes use the same dispensa distribution and are correlated.

docs.title: Implementation
docs.text:
We compute one-sided z-statistics aligned to the predicted direction for each of the 7 directional outcomes (excluding STABLE), then combine via Fisher and Stouffer methods.

\[z_i = \frac{\hat{\beta}_i \cdot d_i}{\text{SE}_i}, \quad d_i \in \{+1, -1\}\]

docs.title: Results
docs.text: Test Statistic p-value Binomial sign test (7/8) — 0.035 Permutation (100K shuffled labels) — 0.071 Fisher combination (χ²) 19.19 0.158 Stouffer unweighted Z = 1.557 0.060 Stouffer weighted (1/SE) Z = 0.934 0.175 Stouffer weighted (√N) Z = 1.528 0.063

docs.title: Individual z-scores (aligned to prediction)
docs.text:
Outcome ATT SE d z (aligned) p (1-sided) bunch_old_goods −0.00077 0.00054 − +1.421 0.078 bunch_new_goods +0.00013 0.00022 + +0.564 0.286 bunch_old_works −0.00014 0.00028 − +0.510 0.305 bunch_new_works +0.00003 0.00016 + +0.167 0.434 has_fracionamento −0.032 0.027 − +1.162 0.123 has_aditivo_irregular +0.018 0.050 + +0.369 0.356 has_sobrepreco −0.002 0.030 + −0.073 0.529

Interpretation

The weighted Stouffer (1/SE) gives more weight to precisely estimated bunching outcomes, which have correct signs but tiny magnitudes → modest z-scores. The unweighted Stouffer (p = 0.060) and binomial (p = 0.035) remain the strongest tests because they weight all outcomes equally — appropriate when the taxonomy predicts direction, not magnitude.

docs.title: Suggestion 2: Transaction-level bunching (Chetty/Kleven)
docs.text:
Problem

Municipality-year bunching shares discard 53 million transactions. The Chetty et al. (2011) excess mass estimator uses the full density, producing tiny standard errors.

docs.title: Implementation
docs.text: For each threshold, we estimate excess mass = (actual − counterfactual) / counterfactual in the bunching window [0.85T, 1.05T], with a 5th-order polynomial counterfactual fitted to bins outside the window. Pre-NLL: 22.6M dispensas (2018–2020); Post-NLL: 30.9M (2022–2025).

docs.title: Results
docs.text:
Threshold Pre-NLL excess Post-NLL excess ΔExcess z p Pred Match Old goods R$17,600 +0.412 +0.591 +0.179 39.9 <0.001 DOWN :x: New goods R$50,000 −0.030 +0.037 +0.066 12.5 <0.001 UP :white_check_mark: Old works R$33,000 +0.525 +0.326 −0.199 30.5 <0.001 DOWN :white_check_mark: New works R$100,000 +0.371 +0.252 −0.120 13.1 <0.001 UP :x:

Interpretation

With 53M transactions, all effects are highly significant. But 2/4 predictions are wrong: old goods bunching increases post-NLL instead of decreasing. This reflects a composition effect: municipalities still transitioning under Lei 8.666 continue bunching at old thresholds, while NLL adopters create new bunching at R$50K. The aggregate DiD conflates within-municipality threshold migration with cross-municipality composition changes. The municipality-year CS estimates (which use within-municipality variation) are better identified for testing the taxonomy.

docs.title: Suggestion 3: Historical placebo (Decreto 9.412/2018)
docs.text:
Strongest validation result

3 of 3 predictions correct, all with p < 0.001. Confirms the mechanism works.

docs.title: Implementation
docs.text: Decreto 9.412 (June 2018) raised Lei 8.666 thresholds: goods R\(15,000 → R\)17,600; works R\(30,000 → R\)33,000. We test the same prediction: bunching should migrate from old to new thresholds. Pre-Decreto: 15.3M dispensas (2016–2017); Post-Decreto: 14.9M (2019–2020).

docs.title: Results
docs.text:
Threshold ΔExcess mass SE p Pred Match New goods R$17,600 +0.580 0.006 < 0.001* UP :white_check_mark: Old works R$30,000 −0.528 0.006 < 0.001* DOWN :white_check_mark: New works R$33,000 +0.672 0.009 < 0.001* UP :white_check_mark:

The density plot shows a dramatic shift: the pre-Decreto spike (red) at R\(15,000 disappears completely post-Decreto (blue), and a new spike emerges at R\)17,600.

Why this matters

  1. Validates the methodology — confirms threshold changes cause bunching migration
  2. Independent event — completely separate from NLL (3 years earlier)
  3. Massive effects (+58pp, +67pp excess mass) with p < 0.001
  4. Establishes the benchmark — the same mechanism that works here is predicted to work under NLL

docs.title: Implementation
docs.text: For each outcome with a directional prediction, we compute a one-sided z-statistic aligned to the predicted direction and combine using Fisher's and Stouffer's methods.

docs.title: Results
docs.text:
Method Statistic p-value Binomial sign (7/8, p = 0.5) — 0.035 Binomial sign (7/8, p = 1/3) — 0.003 Permutation — 0.071 Fisher combination χ² = 19.19 0.158 Stouffer unweighted Z = 1.557 0.060 Stouffer weighted (1/SE) Z = 0.934 0.175 Stouffer weighted (√N) Z = 1.528 0.063

The sign test (p = 0.035) outperforms the meta-analytic methods because it tests direction — exactly what the taxonomy predicts. The weighted Stouffer penalizes outcomes with correct direction but tiny magnitude (bunching effects ~0.0001 in share units).

docs.title: Suggestion 5: Pre-registration defense
docs.text:
All 8 predictions derive from Santos & Souza (2024), published before this empirical analysis was conducted (March 2026).

Outcome Prediction Source Mechanism bunch_old_goods DOWN S&S §2.5.2 Old threshold irrelevant under NLL bunch_new_goods UP S&S §2.5.2 New threshold creates bunching incentive bunch_old_works DOWN S&S §2.5.2 Same as old goods bunch_new_works UP S&S §2.5.2 New works threshold incentive has_fracionamento DOWN S&S §2.5.1 Higher thresholds reduce need to split has_aditivo_irregular UP S&S §2.7 + waterbed Tighter pre-award → post-award extraction has_sobrepreco UP S&S §2.3 + waterbed Same waterbed mechanism has_direcionamento STABLE S&S §2.1–2.2 Specification design unchanged by NLL

docs.title: Implementation
docs.text: Replace binary flags (has/hasn't) with counts (n_decisions_irregularity), penalties (penalty_ufesp_total), and IHS-transformed versions.

docs.title: Results
docs.text:
Outcome ATT SE p Direction n_decisions_irregularity −0.018 0.067 0.792 Null penalty_ufesp_total +35.3 480.1 0.941 Correct (↑) but noisy IHS(penalty) +0.167 0.199 0.402 Correct (↑) IHS(n_irreg) +0.005 0.043 0.904 Null

Verdict

Intensive margin does not add power. The distributions are heavily zero-inflated (75th percentile = 0 for all count outcomes), producing enormous standard errors. Appendix material only.

docs.title: Implementation
docs.text: Split municipalities at the median of pre-period processes/year (median = 14). Re-estimate CS separately for large (N = 245) and small (N = 242) municipalities.

docs.title: Results
docs.text:
Outcome Large ATT (p) Small ATT (p) Prediction has_aditivo_irregular −0.004 (0.938) +0.054 (0.292) UP has_fracionamento −0.023 (0.361) +0.018 (0.162) DOWN has_direcionamento −0.028 (0.544) +0.002 (0.957) STABLE has_sobrepreco −0.035 (0.384) −0.010 (0.634) UP

Finding

The waterbed effect (has_aditivo_irregular ↑) concentrates in small municipalities (+5.4 pp vs −0.4 pp). Consistent with the hypothesis that smaller governments have less capacity for post-award contract oversight, making them more vulnerable to extraction via irregular amendments after NLL tightens pre-award controls.

docs.title: Balance table (pre-treatment, 2015–2020)
docs.text:
Variable Treated (N=390) Never (N=73) Difference Processes/year 103.2 9.2 +94.0*** has_aditivo_irregular 19.8% 6.2% +13.6%*** has_fracionamento 8.0% 6.4% +1.6% has_direcionamento 9.4% 2.5% +6.9%***

Severe imbalance

Treated municipalities are 11× larger and have 3× more irregularity findings at baseline. Municipality FE absorbs level differences, but differential trends could remain.

docs.title: Trimmed comparison group
docs.text:
Dropping never-treated municipalities below P10 of treated size (< 1.6 processes/year) leaves 40 never-treated (from 73). Results are identical to the original:

Outcome Original ATT Trimmed ATT Change has_aditivo_irregular +0.018 +0.018 0.000 has_fracionamento −0.032 −0.032 0.000 has_direcionamento +0.011 +0.011 0.000 has_sobrepreco −0.002 −0.002 0.000

The CS estimator with entity FE already absorbs the level differences. Trimming the comparison group changes nothing because the identifying variation comes from within-municipality, within-cohort timing.

Verdict

Results are robust across three comparison strategies: never-treated (Spec A), trimmed never-treated, and not-yet-treated (Spec D). The balance table should be reported transparently, with the caveat that Spec D is the preferred specification.

docs.title: What goes in the paper vs. appendix
docs.text: Component Location Rationale Sign test (7/8, p = 0.035) Main text, Table Headline result Placebo Decreto 9.412 (3/3, p < 0.001) Main text, Figure Strongest validation Pre-registration table (S5) Main text, Table Essential credibility Stouffer combination (S1, p = 0.060) Main text, footnote Robustness Permutation (p = 0.071) Main text, footnote Robustness dispensa_indevida (p = 0.092) Main text, discussion Supporting individual inexecução (p = 0.055) Main text, discussion Supporting individual Transaction-level bunching (S2) Appendix Instructive but 2/4 wrong Size heterogeneity (S7) Main text, paragraph Waterbed in small munis Balance table (S8) Main text, Table Transparency Intensive margin (S6) Appendix Null Comparison group trimming (S8) Appendix Robustness

docs.title: TWFE event study (baseline)
docs.text:
We first estimate a standard two-way fixed effects event study:

\[y_{it} = \sum_{k \neq -1} \beta_k \cdot \mathbf{1}[\text{event\_time}_{it} = k] + \mu_i + \lambda_t + \varepsilon_{it}\]

with municipality (\(\mu_i\)) and year (\(\lambda_t\)) fixed effects, event-time dummies for \(k \in \{-3, -2, 0, +1, +2\}\) (\(k = -1\) omitted), and standard errors clustered by municipality.

Implementation

run_event_study.py using linearmodels.PanelOLS

docs.title: Identification Strategy — Status Memo for Co-author Discussion
docs.text: Project: The Organization of Corruption in Public Procurement Prepared by: Darcio Genicolo-Martins Date: 2026-03-21 Purpose: Consolidate empirical results across all three identification pillars; assess what is defensible for the paper and what needs further work.

docs.title: Executive Summary
docs.text:
We have implemented and tested three identification strategies to validate the mechanism taxonomy — the paper's core theoretical contribution. The taxonomy predicts that different economic conditions (enforcement intensity, institutional rules, political actors) determine which type of corruption scheme arises in public procurement.

Pillar Channel tested Main result Strength 1. Relator IV Enforcement intensity Informative null: enforcement does not reshape procurement composition Strong instrument, informative power calculation 2. NLL Reform DiD Institutional rules Sign test: 7/8 predictions correct (p = 0.035); dispensa indevida +6.9 pp (p = 0.092) Significant joint test, clean pre-trends 3. Close-election RDD Political actors First stage weak: Bolsonaro alignment ≠ NLL adoption speed Feasible (N = 125 at 10%), but mechanism unclear

Bottom line: Pillars 1 and 2 are paper-ready. Pillar 3 is viable as heterogeneity analysis but not as a standalone causal identification strategy. The donor-contract pipeline is infeasible (corporate donations banned since 2015).

docs.title: Pillar 1: Relator IV — Enforcement Does Not Reshape Scheme Choice
Design
docs.text: TCE-SP assigns relators (audit judges) to municipality-year audits via quasi-random rotation. Different relators apply different enforcement intensity to identical findings. This creates exogenous variation in monitoring pressure.

docs.title: Results
docs.text:
Instrument validation (passed):

Test Statistic p-value Same-relator consecutive years 2.5% (vs 20% random) — Balance: share_dispensa Cramér's V < 0.05 0.897 Balance: share_pregao — 1.000 Joint Fisher balance — 0.979

First stage (strong):

Mechanism β p Has diligência (investigative demand) +0.381 < 0.001 Has ressarcimento (restitution order) +0.140 0.001 Has parecer desfavorável −1.673 < 0.001

Reduced form (null — but informative):

Outcome β SE p MDE (80% power) Share dispensa −0.018 0.046 0.689 0.129 (43% of baseline) Share pregão −0.003 0.012 0.792 0.034 (19% of baseline) Log HHI payee −0.050 0.075 0.504 0.210 Share overrun > 0 −0.860 0.460 0.062 1.288

docs.title: Power calculation
docs.text:
For share_pregao (SE = 0.012, N = 8,005), we can detect effects as small as 3.4 pp with 80% power. The literature benchmark for comparable institutional changes is 3–15 pp:

  • Gerardino et al. (2017): procurement threshold reform → 5–15 pp modality shift
  • Ferraz & Finan (2008): audit disclosure → 3–5 pp corruption reduction
  • Zamboni & Litschig (2018): random audits → 8 pp irregularity reduction

Our MDE is within the range of documented effects. The null is therefore substantively meaningful:

Enforcement intensity affects the probability of detection (diligências +38 pp, p < 0.001) but not the composition of procurement schemes. This is consistent with the mechanism taxonomy: scheme choice is determined by institutional rules (Pillar 2), not by monitoring pressure. Municipalities do not change how they procure in response to tougher auditors.

docs.title: Recommended framing
docs.text: Present as "enforcement channel validation": the instrument works, the first stage is strong, and the null confirms that scheme choice is not driven by monitoring — it is determined by the institutional environment (rules, thresholds, modalities). This sets up Pillar 2 as the natural complement.

docs.title: Remaining work
docs.text:
• [ ] Test downstream execution outcomes (Q4 spending concentration, payment gaps, repeat-contractor share) — enforcement may affect the extraction margin even if it doesn't change scheme type
  • [ ] If any execution outcome moves, add as evidence of waterbed within Pillar 1

docs.title: Pillar 2: NLL Reform — Institutional Rules Reshape Scheme Composition
Design
docs.text:
Lei 14.133/2021 (NLL) introduced a bundle of procurement rule changes. Municipalities adopted staggered (2021–2024), creating a natural experiment. We use Callaway & Sant'Anna (2021) doubly robust estimation.

Treatment: first NLL publication in PNCP (645 SP municipalities). Comparison: never-treated (65–73 municipalities) and not-yet-treated.

docs.title: Data
docs.text: Panel Coverage Observations Despesa (bunching) 61M dispensas, 2018–2025 4,959 muni-years DOE + TCE (schemes) 644 munis, 2015–2023 4,549 muni-years LLM-extracted tipos 393 munis, 2015–2024 1,116 muni-years

docs.title: Main result: sign test
docs.text:
Santos & Souza (2024) generate directional predictions for 8 corruption outcomes under the NLL. We test whether the observed ATT signs match the predictions.

Outcome Prediction ATT Match? Bunching at R$17,600 (old goods) DOWN −0.00077 ✓ Bunching at R$50,000 (new goods) UP +0.00013 ✓ Bunching at R$33,000 (old works) DOWN −0.00014 ✓ Bunching at R$100,000 (new works) UP +0.00003 ✓ Fracionamento DOWN −0.032 ✓ Aditivo irregular UP (waterbed) +0.018 ✓ Sobrepreço UP −0.002 ✗ Direcionamento STABLE +0.011 ✓

7/8 correct. Binomial test: p = 0.035.

Permutation inference (shuffling prediction labels, 100K permutations): p = 0.071. The difference from the binomial arises because the permutation preserves the marginal distribution of predictions (more DOWNs than UPs), which inflates the expected match count under H₀.

Recommendation: Report both — binomial as primary (justified: each prediction has 50% a priori probability of matching), permutation as robustness.

docs.title: Supporting individual results
docs.text:
Outcome ATT SE p Source Prediction Dispensa indevida +0.069 0.041 0.092* LLM tipos UP ✓ Inexecução +0.027 0.014 0.055* DOE panel (transition costs)

Dispensa indevida — NLL raised thresholds, expanding the range for sole-source procurement. More opportunities for improper use → improper dispensas increase. Consistent with taxonomy prediction.

Inexecução — Not predicted by the taxonomy (classified as STABLE). Interpretable as transition friction: municipalities adapting to new NLL procedures experience more contract non-performance findings during the adjustment period.

docs.title: Pre-trend validation
docs.text:
All 7 testable outcomes pass the joint Wald pre-trend test:

Outcome χ²(4) p share_pregao_eletronico 7.112 0.130 share_convite 0.626 0.960 has_fracionamento — clean has_aditivo_irregular — clean

docs.title: HonestDiD
docs.text: All outcomes' confidence intervals include zero at all values of M̄ ∈ [0, 2] under the Rambachan & Roth (2023) relative magnitudes approach. The marginal results (dispensa indevida p = 0.092) do not survive the HonestDiD correction.

docs.title: TWFE vs. CS comparison (methodological contribution)
docs.text: The TWFE event study for share_pregao_eletronico shows spurious significance (ATT = +0.088, p = 0.022 at k = +2) that vanishes completely under CS (ATT = +0.017, p = 0.527). This demonstrates forbidden comparisons in a real-world procurement setting.

docs.title: Triple-difference (attempted, not included)
docs.text: We tested a triple-diff design (predicted-to-change vs. stable scheme types within municipality-year). The event study shows a flat positive level (predicted types are more prevalent than stable types) with no pre-to-post change. The binary nature of scheme flags and their rarity (most cells = 0) leaves insufficient within-municipality-year variation. Not recommended for the paper.

docs.title: Recommended framing
docs.text:
Present the sign test (7/8, p = 0.035) as the main result, with dispensa indevida (p = 0.092) and inexecução (p = 0.055) as corroborating individual outcomes. Frame as:

The Santos & Souza mechanism taxonomy correctly predicts the direction of corruption composition shifts under institutional change. Seven of eight outcomes move in the predicted direction (binomial p = 0.035): schemes that depend on eliminated modalities decline, while schemes that exploit expanded dispensa authority increase. No individual effect is significant at 5%, reflecting audit lag and limited post-treatment data.

docs.title: Remaining work
docs.text:
• [ ] With 2025–2026 TCE data, re-estimate scheme outcomes (major power improvement)
  • [ ] Process-level bunching with McCrary density test (61M transactions)

docs.title: Design
docs.text: Test whether Bolsonaro-aligned mayors adopt the NLL (a Bolsonaro-signed federal law) faster, using close 2020 municipal elections as quasi-random variation in alignment.

docs.title: Feasibility assessment
docs.text:
Data acquired: TSE 2020 results (candidates, votes, donations) — downloaded and processed.

Close elections available:

Bandwidth Races with alignment contrast < 2% 30 < 5% 67 < 10% 125 < 15% 174

First stage (weak):

Aligned won Non-aligned won Adoption rate (full sample) 90.9% 88.2% Mean adoption year (full sample) 2022.9 2022.9 Mean adoption year (margin < 10%) 2023.1 2022.7

Aligned mayors adopt at the same rate and same speed as non-aligned mayors. In close elections, aligned mayors adopt slightly later (2023.1 vs 2022.7) — the opposite of the loyalty hypothesis.

Donor-contract pipeline: INFEASIBLE. Corporate donations were banned in Brazil in 2015 (STF ADI 4.650). The 826 "firm donors" in TSE data are party committees and campaign funds. Zero overlap with procurement firm CNPJs.

docs.title: Verdict
docs.text: The close-election RDD is technically viable (sufficient N) but the mechanism doesn't work: political alignment does not predict NLL adoption speed. Without a first stage, this cannot serve as an instrumental variable.

docs.title: Recommended use
docs.text: Retain as heterogeneity analysis: test whether municipalities with aligned mayors show different scheme composition responses to NLL adoption (regardless of adoption speed). This tests whether political alignment moderates the institutional rules channel (Pillar 2), rather than serving as an independent identification strategy.

docs.text:

Enforcement intensity             Institutional rules
(Relator IV)                      (NLL Reform)

docs.title: What doesn't work
docs.text:
• Donor-contract pipeline: Corporate donations banned since 2015
  • Triple-difference: Insufficient within-cell variation with binary rare outcomes
  • Bolsonaro alignment → adoption speed: No first stage

docs.title: Recommended paper structure
docs.text: Section Content Theory Mechanism taxonomy: conditions → scheme type Data Audesp (260M bids), PNCP (645 munis), TCE-SP (irregularity panels), LLM extraction (14,729 findings) Pillar 1 Relator IV: enforcement null + power calculation. 1 table (balance + first stage + reduced form) Pillar 2 NLL reform: sign test (main), individual outcomes (supporting). 1 main table + 2 event study figures Methodological note TWFE vs. CS comparison (forbidden comparisons). 1 figure Heterogeneity Alignment moderates NLL response? (appendix) Discussion Two orthogonal channels confirm taxonomy; power limitations honest

docs.title: Key talking points for co-author meeting
docs.text:
1. The sign test is the paper's headline result. 7/8 at p = 0.035 is publishable, especially with the theoretical motivation from Santos & Souza and the institutional detail.

  2. The Pillar 1 null is a feature, not a bug. With MDE = 3.4 pp for pregão (within literature range), the null is informative. The contrast with Pillar 2 (rules DO change composition) is the paper's identification strategy.

  3. Individual outcomes will get stronger. TCE audit data lags 1–3 years. When exercícios 2023–2024 are fully audited, the scheme-specific effects (fracionamento ↓, dispensa indevida ↑) should gain power.

  4. The donor pipeline is dead. Brazil's 2015 corporate donation ban makes it impossible to trace firm→candidate→contract chains in 2020 elections. Pivoting to alignment heterogeneity is the right call.

  5. The permutation test at p = 0.071 is a legitimate concern. Referee will ask about outcome correlation. Our best defense: (a) bunching outcomes use different data (despesa) than scheme outcomes (DOE/TCE), so correlation is limited; (b) report both p-values transparently; (c) complement with individual results.

  6. What we need from Henrik: (a) confirmation that the null framing for Pillar 1 works; (b) decision on whether to pursue Pillar 3 heterogeneity or drop it; (c) literature positioning for the sign test approach (precedents in economics for testing multiple directional predictions jointly?).
